title: MLOps for Small Teams: You Don't Need a Giant Infrastructure
author: Rohit Ghumare
excerpt: Big tech's MLOps stack is overkill for most teams. Here's how to build a lean pipeline that actually ships.
content:
The Small Team Advantage

Read the MLOps literature and you'd think every team needs a feature store, a model registry, a Kubernetes cluster, and a dedicated platform engineer. The reality for small teams is different. You have three people, a tight budget, and a model that needs to be in production by Friday.

The good news: you don't need the full stack. You need the right slice of it.

The Minimal Viable Pipeline

Start with four components:

  1. Versioned training code. Git is enough. Tag your commits with model versions.
  2. A reproducible environment. A requirements.txt and a Dockerfile go a long way. If you can't reproduce the training, you're not doing ML — you're doing hope.
  3. An artifact store. S3, GCS, or even a network drive. Save model weights, datasets, and config files together.
  4. A serving endpoint. FastAPI in a Docker container behind a reverse proxy. That's production for most use cases.

Skip These (For Now)

  • Feature stores. Build one when you have multiple models sharing features and drift is killing you. Not before.
  • Complex orchestration. GitHub Actions or a simple cron job is fine until you have dozens of pipelines.
  • Auto-scaling clusters. Start with a single VM. Scale when latency or throughput becomes a real problem.
  • Experiment tracking platforms. A shared spreadsheet or a simple JSON log works until you have more experiments than you can remember.

What You Should Not Skip

  • Input validation. Your model will receive garbage. Make sure it fails gracefully.
  • Logging. Log inputs, outputs, and errors. Not for compliance — for your own sanity when something breaks.
  • Rollback. Keep the previous model version one command away from redeployment.
  • A single source of truth for configs. Environment variables or a simple YAML file. Avoid hardcoded paths and API keys in notebooks.

The Mindset Shift

MLOps at small teams is not about tooling — it's about habits. Commit often. Document assumptions. Test inference before deploying. Monitor in production. These habits cost nothing and save everything.

Start small. Ship fast. Iterate. The fancy platform can wait.
